entities.name: PEARL MILLET
entities.description: A cereal grain, Pennisetum glaucum (L.) R. Br., studied for drought tolerance.

entities.name: DROUGHT TOLERANCE
entities.description: The ability of pearl millet to withstand terminal drought conditions.

entities.name: TRANSPIRATION
entities.description: The process of water movement through a plant and its evaporation from aerial parts, such as leaves.

entities.name: ABA
entities.description: Abscisic acid, a plant hormone, measured in leaves.

entities.name: VAPOUR PRESSURE DEFICIT
entities.description: A measure of the dryness of the air, VPD.

entities.name: TRANSPIRATION EFFICIENCY
entities.description: A measure of water use efficiency, TE.

entities.name: QTL
entities.description: Quantitative trait locus, a region of DNA associated with a trait of interest.

entities.name: NIL-QTLS
entities.description: Near-isogenic lines introgressed with a terminal drought tolerance QTL.

entities.name: WATER STRESS
entities.description: Conditions where water availability is limited.

entities.name: GRAIN FILLING
entities.description: The process of nutrient accumulation in developing grains.

entities.name: DRY WEIGHT
entities.description: The weight of a plant or plant part after removing all moisture.

relationships.description: Pearl millet is being studied for its drought tolerance.
Transpiration rate is measured in pearl millet.
ABA levels are measured in pearl millet leaves.
Transpiration is measured at different vapour pressure deficits in pearl millet.
Drought tolerance is associated with a QTL in pearl millet.
Drought tolerant lines have lower transpiration rates.
Drought tolerant lines have higher ABA levels.
Transpiration may be related to leaf ABA levels.
Transpiration is affected by vapour pressure deficit.
NIL-QTLS carry a terminal drought tolerance QTL.
ABA levels do not increase under water stress.
Drought tolerance is critical for grain filling.
Differences in dry weight contribute to water saving in drought tolerant lines.
Water saving due to reduced transpiration contributes to dry weight increase.
